name: Bordeaux Right Bank to Left Bank Loop
name_original: Boucle de Bordeaux de rive droite à gauche
description: This 47.7-mile (76.7 km) moderate mountain biking loop explores both banks of the Garonne River around Bordeaux.
region_intro: Mountain biking around the Garonne-Kanal offers diverse terrain, from the flat, shaded paths alongside the canal to more challenging, hilly areas in the surrounding region. The main canal path, while generally smooth, can feature unpaved sections with natural obstacles, providing varied conditions for mountain bikers. The landscape transitions from serene greenways and agricultural fields to areas with more significant elevation changes, particularly where the Garonne River flows from the Pyrenees. This region is characterized by its blend of riverine environments, historic towns, and rural French countryside. Best mountain bike trails around the Garonne-Kanal The most popular mountain bike trail is Bordeaux Right Bank to Left Bank Loop, a 47.7 miles (76.7 km) moderate trail that takes 4 hours 57 minutes to complete. This route explores the urban and riverine landscapes around Bordeaux, a key city along the Garonne. Another top favourite among local mountain bikers is Fallen Oak Tree Obstacle – Lèze River loop from Portet Saint-Simon, a difficult 23.4 miles (37.7 km) path. This route offers a challenging ride through varied terrain near the Lèze River, a tributary of the Garonne. Local mountain bikers also love the Trail along the Garona River – Aiguamòg Reservoir loop from Vielha, a 25.3 miles (40.8 km) trail leading through mountainous river valleys, often completed in about 4 hours 8 minutes. Mountain biking around the Garonne-Kanal is defined by shaded greenways, rural countryside with fields and vineyards, and the impressive engineering structures of the canal itself. The network offers options for different ability levels, from flat canal-side paths to more demanding ascents in the surrounding hills. The routes in Garonne canal are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 700 reviews. More than 7,800 mountain bikers have used komoot to explore Garonne canal's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many mountain bike trails are available around the Garonne Canal?
A: There are over 430 mountain bike trails around the Garonne Canal. These routes cater to various skill levels, with approximately 100 easy, over 230 moderate, and nearly 90 difficult options to explore.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on mountain bike trails near the Garonne Canal?
A: The terrain varies significantly. Along the main canal, paths are often flat and shaded, though some sections can be unpaved with natural obstacles like tree roots. Further from the canal, especially where the Garonne River flows from the Pyrenees, you'll find more challenging, hilly areas with significant elevation changes, offering a more demanding mountain biking experience.

Q: Are there any family-friendly mountain bike trails around the Garonne Canal?
A: Yes, the Garonne Canal region offers many family-friendly options, particularly on the flatter, shaded sections directly alongside the canal. These paths are generally traffic-free and suitable for relaxed riding. For a moderate option, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e999432511/bordeaux-right-bank-to-left-bank-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Bordeaux Right Bank to Left Bank Loop&lt;/a&gt;, which is 76.7 km long and explores urban and riverine landscapes.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the mountain bike trails?
A: Many trails in the Garonne Canal region are suitable for cycling with dogs, especially the wider, less technical paths along the canal. However, it's always recommended to check local regulations for specific areas or nature reserves, and ensure your dog is well-behaved and on a leash where required.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or attractions I can see while mountain biking in the area?
A: The region is rich in historical and natural attractions. You can encounter ingenious engineering structures like the Agen Aqueduct and the unique water slope in Montech. Historic towns such as Moissac, with its ancient Abbey of St-Pierre, and Bordeaux, a UNESCO World Heritage city, are also along the route. For natural beauty, you'll pass through orchards, vineyards, and fields. You can also explore nearby &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3302745" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Lake Major of Colomers&lt;/a&gt; or historical sites like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1025678" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Château du Hâ&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there circular mountain bike routes available?
A: Yes, there are several circular mountain bike routes. For a challenging option in the mountainous river valleys, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e951922760/salardu-vielha-salto-del-pish-varrados-bagergue-ruta-circular-por-el-valle-de-aran" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Salardú - Vielha - Salto del Pish - Varradós - Bagergue — Ruta circular por el Valle de Arán&lt;/a&gt;, which is 45.7 km long with significant elevation gain.

Q: What is the best time of year for mountain biking around the Garonne Canal?
A: The Garonne Canal is enjoyable for cycling for much of the year. Spring and autumn offer pleasant temperatures and beautiful scenery. Even during warmer months, the main canal path is largely shaded by plane trees, providing a comfortable environment. However, some unpaved sections might be more challenging after heavy rain.

Q: How do other mountain bikers rate the trails in the Garonne Canal region?
A: The mountain bike trails around the Garonne Canal are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 700 reviews. More than 7,800 mountain bikers have used komoot to explore the region's varied terrain, often praising the blend of serene greenways, rural countryside, and impressive engineering structures.

Q: Are there challenging mountain bike trails with significant elevation gain?
A: Yes, while the main canal path is mostly flat, the surrounding region, particularly towards the Pyrenees, offers challenging routes with substantial elevation gain. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e950378865/vielha-pista-salient-bords-mont-vilac-vielha-centro-btt-del-valle-de-aran" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vielha - Pista Salient - Bords - Mont - Vilac - Vielha — Centro BTT del Valle de Arán&lt;/a&gt; route is a difficult 38.1 km trail with over 1500 meters of ascent.

Q: What are the options for parking and public transport access to the mountain bike trails?
A: Many towns and villages along the Garonne Canal offer parking facilities, making it convenient to access the trails. The canal also connects to major cities like Bordeaux and Agen, which are well-served by public transport, including train stations. This allows for flexible planning, whether you're looking to start a multi-day tour or a shorter loop.

Q: Are there any technical singletrack sections for advanced mountain bikers?
A: While the main canal path is not typically singletrack, some off-canal detours and routes in the more mountainous areas, particularly those described as 'difficult' on komoot, can feature unpaved sections with obstacles and more technical terrain. For a difficult ride with varied terrain, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e952277185/mtb-route-21-salardu-prueedo-arties" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;MTB Route 21: Salardú - Prüedo - Arties&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Where can I find cafes or accommodation along the mountain bike routes?
A: The towns and villages situated along the Garonne Canal, such as Agen, Moissac, Montech, and Castelsarrasin, offer numerous opportunities for cafes, restaurants, and various accommodation options. These places are ideal for rest stops, meals, and overnight stays, allowing you to experience local gastronomy and culture.
tips: Superb ruined castle in the middle of paths and tall grass. Vegetation has taken over the castle and you can see trees growing on the roof. It takes its name from the plot where it was built. But its origin remains mysterious! All the more reason to let yourself be haunted by this building.
Single passable without ruts, nice and easily accessible from the Lapébie track and a few alleys!
The fording to be done thoroughly like kids! Even in winter in the cold it goes and it's funny!
Nice single, very flat (like the whole area here!) but still interesting! Overlooks the airfield runway
Single that runs along the runway of the Saucats aerodrome! Joined on both sides by well rolling gravel tracks. A pleasure to see the planes take off!

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this mountain bike loop?
faqs.answer: The Boucle de Bordeaux de rive droite à gauche is a substantial 76.7-kilometer route. For an average mountain biker, it typically takes around 5 hours to complete, depending on your pace and how often you stop to enjoy the scenery.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this mountain bike route?
faqs.answer: You'll encounter a diverse range of terrain, characteristic of the Bordeaux region. Expect a mix of gentle hills, extensive pine forests, and vineyards. There are well-practicable singletrack sections, stony paths, non-bituminous passages, and gravel tracks. You might even splash through a stream ford!

faqs.question: Is this trail suitable for beginner mountain bikers?
faqs.answer: This route is generally considered to be of moderate difficulty. While the overall loop is manageable for riders with good physical fitness, some sections, particularly singletrack or climbs like the 'côte de Bouliac' (with an average gradient of 6.5% over 900 meters), might require excellent technical mastery. Beginners might find certain parts challenging.

faqs.question: Where can I start this loop and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: As a loop trail, you can start at various points. While specific parking areas aren't detailed, Bordeaux offers numerous public parking options. Consider starting near one of the parks along the Garonne, such as Parc des berges des Queyries or Parc des Angéliques, which often have nearby parking facilities.

faqs.question: What are the notable sights or natural features along the way?
faqs.answer: The loop offers extensive views of Bordeaux and its surroundings. You'll pass by the ruins of Château du Dragon, enjoy singletrack sections like Madirac Singletrack and Singletrack in the Pines, and even ride Singletrack Along Saucats Airfield where you might see planes. The route also traverses vineyards, parks, and areas within the 'Réserve naturelle géologique de Saucats et La Brède'.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to ride this mountain bike loop?
faqs.answer: Spring and autumn are generally the best times to ride this loop. The weather is usually milder, and the natural scenery, from vineyards to forests, is particularly beautiful. Summer can be quite hot, while winter might bring wetter conditions, making some unpaved sections muddy.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or fees required to ride this trail?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to ride the Boucle de Bordeaux de rive droite à gauche. It's a public route accessible to all.

faqs.question: Is the Boucle de Bordeaux de rive droite à gauche dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Yes, dogs are generally allowed on this trail. However, as parts of the route pass through urban areas, parks, and potentially protected natural zones, it's always best to keep your dog on a leash and ensure you clean up after them. Be mindful of other trail users, especially in busier sections.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other official cycling paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this extensive loop intersects with several other official cycling paths. You'll find sections that overlap with routes like Étape 7 • Chemin d'Amadour, EV3 France 39, Créon - Bordeaux, Via Sancti Martini - Chemin de Saragosse, and Le Tour de Gironde à Vélo - Étape 1 (Bordeaux - Sauveterre-de-Guyenne), among others.

faqs.question: Are there any specific challenging sections I should be aware of?
faqs.answer: Yes, while mostly moderate, be prepared for the climb up the 'côte de Bouliac' on the right bank. It has an average gradient of 6.5% over 900 meters, which requires sustained effort, especially without electric assistance. Some singletrack sections also demand good bike handling skills.

faqs.question: Are there places to stop for food or water along the route?
faqs.answer: Given that the loop traverses both urban areas of Bordeaux and smaller towns, you will find opportunities to stop for food and water. It's always wise to carry sufficient supplies, especially for the longer, more rural sections, but you can expect to pass through areas with cafes and shops.
